caveats: a: No source measures wage-weighted exposure specifically for this six-digit industry.
a: Generic distributor software evidence does not capture tactile, color-sensitive, or trend-driven textile decisions.
a: The supplied compensation ratio has a vintage mismatch and cross-industry harmonization adjustment.
rho: The sources are not specific to piece goods and notions wholesalers.
rho: Vendor claims describe target performance rather than independently audited outcomes.
rho: Implementation may improve speed and assortment quality without producing equivalent payroll reduction.
e: NAICS activity definitions do not establish size, independence, recurring account quality, or transferability.
e: Census notes that some wholesale establishments are connected to one manufacturer, which may make them captive or economically dependent.
e: The supplied firm count is estimate-quality and margin-bridged from a broad distributor benchmark.
s5: Gallup reports intentions rather than completed deals and includes nonqualifying forms of transfer.
s5: Wholesale marketplace observations are not specific to textiles and lack an eligible-firm denominator.
s5: Supplier exclusivity and owner-led taste or customer relationships may not survive a sale.
q: No source directly measures retained automation benefit in this industry.
q: Tariffs, freight, exchange rates, inventory write-downs, and fashion mix can overwhelm the observable margin effect.
q: Retention likely differs sharply between exclusive decorative fabrics and commodity thread or notions.
d5: The code spans product markets with materially different cycles and end users.
d5: Apparel manufacturing output is a downstream proxy and excludes imported finished-goods effects and nonapparel fabric demand.
d5: The early-2025 import increase may reflect tariff timing and does not establish sustainable merchant-wholesaler volume.
o: Digital ordering can remove human touches while leaving the wholesaler as inventory owner and contractual principal.
o: Large manufacturers can source direct more readily than small workrooms, designers, retailers, and craft businesses.
o: Exclusive design lines and tactile sampling retain more operator need than standardized notions.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Transactional product resale margin supported by repeat accounts and service embedded in assortment curation, sampling, color and lot matching, import sourcing, local stock, breaking bulk, credit, order handling, and delivery; customers pay per order under catalog, quote, or account pricing.
lens.excluded_activities: Textile mills and converters, manufacturers' captive sales offices, commission agents without inventory or fulfillment control, consumer-only retailers, industrial-yarn wholesalers, passive import entities, and firms whose supplier or customer relationships cannot transfer independently of an owner.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market merchant wholesalers that repeatedly source, hold, sample, quote, sell, and replenish piece goods, apparel and upholstery fabrics, knitting yarns, thread, notions, trimmings, sewing accessories, and hair accessories for external manufacturers, workrooms, designers, retailers, institutions, and craft businesses.

a.bridge: Distribution Strategy Group and B2Sell address wholesale distribution broadly rather than piece-goods firms, and they document sector use or technical capability rather than a wage-weighted task share. I map order intake, invoices, RFQs, remittances, product search, sales preparation, demand forecasting, inventory analysis, and routine service to exposed work, while assigning less exposure to physical handling, sample preparation, tactile quality assessment, relationship selling, and supplier negotiation. The proxy may overstate firms with weak product data but understate image-based fabric search and trend analysis.
caveats: No source measures wage-weighted exposure specifically for this six-digit industry.
Generic distributor software evidence does not capture tactile, color-sensitive, or trend-driven textile decisions.
The supplied compensation ratio has a vintage mismatch and cross-industry harmonization adjustment.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from representative NAICS 424310 firms, including time studies for sampling, product search, quoting, order entry, purchasing, customer service, warehouse, and delivery work, would materially change exposure.
a.rationale: This trade has many repetitive commercial and administrative workflows plus a large, visual, long-tail SKU catalog. AI can structure emailed orders, search product attributes, draft quotes, reconcile documents, prioritize sales activity, and forecast stock. Human work remains important for fabric hand, color and lot judgment, physical samples, creative customer guidance, warehouse work, and relationship-based sourcing.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production for US apparel and leather manufacturing measures one important downstream user, not the full constant-price basket sold through NAICS 424310. Its index fell from 92.98 in 2023 to 77.80 in 2025. OTEXA measures broad import volumes and showed textile imports 14.2% higher in early 2025, but those flows include finished and industrial products and may bypass merchants. I combine domestic manufacturing contraction with resilient imported supply and diversified upholstery, automotive, craft, hair-accessory, and institutional uses. This may overstate decline where wholesalers serve nonapparel niches and understate channel loss to direct import.
caveats: The code spans product markets with materially different cycles and end users.
Apparel manufacturing output is a downstream proxy and excludes imported finished-goods effects and nonapparel fabric demand.
The early-2025 import increase may reflect tariff timing and does not establish sustainable merchant-wholesaler volume.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted NAICS 424310 sales and physical yardage or unit shipments by apparel fabric, upholstery, automotive textile, yarn, notions, and hair accessories, together with merchant-channel share and customer industry, would materially change demand.
d5.rationale: Domestic garment production is a structural headwind to local fabric and trim demand, and volatile fashion cycles create inventory risk. Imported fabrics, reshoring niches, upholstery, automotive uses, crafts, and low-minimum local replenishment prevent a simple collapse. The base therefore assumes modest real contraction rather than disappearance.

caveats: NAICS activity definitions do not establish size, independence, recurring account quality, or transferability.
Census notes that some wholesale establishments are connected to one manufacturer, which may make them captive or economically dependent.
The supplied firm count is estimate-quality and margin-bridged from a broad distributor benchmark.
e.change_evidence: A commercial business file matched to ownership, revenue, EBITDA, manufacturer affiliation, inventory ownership, repeat-account share, channel mix, warehouse operations, and management depth would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The code is explicitly merchant wholesale distribution, and fabrics and notions are repeatedly ordered by external manufacturing, design, workroom, retail, and craft customers. Eligibility is reduced for manufacturer-linked sales branches, import entities that merely book transactions, spot traders, consumer-oriented sellers, firms outside the operating size band despite the dataset bridge, and owner-dependent businesses whose curation or supplier ties are not transferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 424310 ($1,608,182,791) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 424310 ($16,228,182,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $688,890 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 424310); class <5: 1,739 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,334,601 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $151,744 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 400 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,949,838 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $449,097 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 231 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,577,100 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $747,816 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 194 firms, avg revenue/firm $20,961,773 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,383,354 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 55 firms, avg revenue/firm $56,515,418 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,425,803 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $181,656,650 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $20,654,361 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 249

caveats: Digital ordering can remove human touches while leaving the wholesaler as inventory owner and contractual principal.
Large manufacturers can source direct more readily than small workrooms, designers, retailers, and craft businesses.
Exclusive design lines and tactile sampling retain more operator need than standardized notions.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews by account size, direct-import penetration, manufacturer-direct sales, marketplace share, order-level sample and cut-length usage, minimum-order requirements, and churn after digital portal adoption would materially change operator need.
o.rationale: Many customers still need an accountable operator to curate assortments, provide samples, hold local inventory, break mill minimums, match colors and lots, extend credit, and resolve quality or delivery problems. Digital catalogs, marketplaces, direct overseas sourcing, manufacturer sales offices, and customer self-service can remove an independent merchant from standardized purchases, so operator need is materially lower than physical product demand.

caveats: No source directly measures retained automation benefit in this industry.
Tariffs, freight, exchange rates, inventory write-downs, and fashion mix can overwhelm the observable margin effect.
Retention likely differs sharply between exclusive decorative fabrics and commodity thread or notions.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level gross-margin cohorts, renewal and quote histories, direct-import alternatives, product exclusivity, service-level data, win-loss records, and implemented labor savings across several independent fabric distributors would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Automation can lower selling and administrative cost, reduce inventory mistakes, improve conversion, and avoid hiring, but the benefit sits inside negotiated product margins. Customers can compare imports and digital catalogs, direct sourcing is feasible at scale, and competitors can adopt similar tools. Retention is stronger where proprietary lines, immediate stock, sampling, small minimums, color matching, and dependable service make price only one part of the purchase decision.

rho.bridge: Broad distributor surveys report current use and expected workforce effects, while B2Sell reports concrete workflows and vendor success criteria; neither measures five-year implementation in this code. I translate active adoption and mature document-processing designs into moderate implementation, reduced for fragmented ERP systems, unstructured product attributes, small-firm budgets, supplier variability, image and color accuracy, and change management. An acquirer's shared product master and platform could outperform the proxy.
caveats: The sources are not specific to piece goods and notions wholesalers.
Vendor claims describe target performance rather than independently audited outcomes.
Implementation may improve speed and assortment quality without producing equivalent payroll reduction.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year production cohorts from textile distributors and their ERP, product-information, and order-automation vendors showing deployment rates, exception shares, forecast error, labor hours, and customer-service outcomes would materially change implementation.
rho.rationale: Order and accounting workflows can be implemented with human exception queues, and the pressure from digital competitors creates a reason to act. Implementation is harder in assortment and forecasting because short product histories, seasonality, fashion shifts, multiple units of measure, dye lots, and supplier lead times weaken model reliability.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year plans among all US employer-business owners, with 22% planning to sell or transfer, rather than completed control transfers among eligible piece-goods wholesalers. I reduce the center for gifts, internal transfers, failed sales, owner-dependent supplier relationships, obsolete inventory, and uncertain demand. BizBuySell confirms wholesale businesses transact but represents smaller marketplace deals rather than this code. The bridge may understate strategic purchases of attractive specialty assortments and overstate completion of intended exits.
caveats: Gallup reports intentions rather than completed deals and includes nonqualifying forms of transfer.
Wholesale marketplace observations are not specific to textiles and lack an eligible-firm denominator.
Supplier exclusivity and owner-led taste or customer relationships may not survive a sale.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data from PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, GF Data, textile-industry advisers, and state business records linked to an eligible NAICS 424310 firm denominator would materially change transfer probability.
s5.rationale: Aging owners create a plausible supply of firms, and inventory, customer accounts, supplier relationships, and operating staff can transfer. Yet fashion and inventory risk, dependence on principals, and a shrinking domestic apparel-production base can impair saleability and financing.

principal_driver: Large catalogs and repetitive quote, order, inventory, and customer-service workflows create a concrete automation opportunity in well-run specialists.
principal_weakness: Domestic downstream contraction and easy direct or digital sourcing weaken durable demand for an independent merchant intermediary.

run_meta.title: Piece Goods, Notions, and Other Dry Goods Merchant Wholesalers

sources.supports: Census defines NAICS 424310 as merchant wholesale distribution of piece goods, fabrics, nonindustrial knitting yarns, thread, notions, and hair accessories; supports lens scope and product heterogeneity.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS: Piece Goods, Notions, and Other Dry Goods Merchant Wholesalers

sources.supports: Explains that merchant wholesalers typically take title, maintain warehouses, handle goods for customers, and may be connected to a single manufacturer; supports operator functions, eligibility, and captive-channel caveats.
sources.title: Wholesale Trade Census Bureau Profile

sources.supports: Reports nearly three-quarters of distributor teams use generative AI daily, with strongest automation efficiencies in fulfillment, warehousing, order-to-cash, and sales, and potential sector workforce reduction of one-third or more over five years; supports broad-distribution exposure and implementation proxies.
sources.title: The 2025 State of AI in Distribution

sources.supports: Documents automation of distributor invoices, purchase orders, bills of lading, RFQs, remittances, ERP validation, and human exception routing, with vendor criteria of at least 80% straight-through processing and at least 95% field extraction accuracy; supports task feasibility and implementation caveats.
sources.title: AI Document Processing for Distributors

sources.supports: Survey of 300 distributors, 299 suppliers, and 599 customers documents omnichannel expectations, trust and reliability, scale advantages, AI-enabled procurement and sales workflows, and changing distributor competition; supports exposure, retention, and operator-need judgments.
sources.title: AI in Distribution: Driving Digital Transformation Today

sources.supports: Federal Reserve industrial-production series reports the real output index declining from 92.98 in 2023 to 82.32 in 2024 and 77.80 in 2025; supports a downside proxy for domestic apparel-related fabric demand.
sources.title: Industrial Production: Apparel and Leather Goods

sources.supports: Reports early-2025 US textile and apparel imports of 24,550.1 million square-meter equivalents, with textile imports up 14.2% and apparel imports up 9.2% from the prior year; supports imported-flow resilience and tariff-timing caveats.
sources.title: Office of Textiles and Apparel March 2025 Trade Data Release

sources.supports: Fall 2024 survey of 1,264 US owners reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; supports the transfer proxy and intention-versus-completion caveat.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports observed US wholesale and distribution business sales and transaction metrics for 2021 through 2025; confirms an active transfer market while showing marketplace selection toward smaller businesses.
sources.title: Wholesale and Distribution Business Valuation Multiples and Financial Benchmarks

title: Piece Goods, Notions, and Other Dry Goods Merchant Wholesalers
